items.title: USDA updates SNAP Food Restriction Waivers list: Texas (Apr. 1), Florida (Apr. 20), Colorado (Apr. 30) and new long-dated waivers (e.g., Kansas Feb. 15, 2027; Nevada Feb. 1, 2028)
items.summary: USDA’s SNAP Food Restriction Waivers page lists multiple state implementation dates, including Texas starting 04/01/26 (restricts sweetened drinks and candy), Florida starting 04/20/26 (restricts soda, energy drinks, candy, and prepared desserts), and Colorado starting 04/30/26 (restricts soft drinks). The same USDA list also shows recently approved waivers with later start dates, including Kansas (02/15/27) restricting candy and soft drinks and Nevada (02/01/28) restricting candy and sugar-sweetened beverages.

items.title: USDA: Secretary Rollins signs four new SNAP restriction waivers (Kansas, Nevada, Ohio, Wyoming) and previews retailer stocking standards final rule
items.summary: In a March 4, 2026 press release, USDA says Secretary Brooke Rollins signed four new SNAP restriction waivers for Kansas, Nevada, Ohio, and Wyoming. USDA also says a SNAP retailer “Stocking Standards” final rule is impending and would raise the minimum staple-food stocking requirements for stores that accept SNAP.

items.title: CMS: Medicaid/CHIP renewals in Oct. 2025—71% renewed (49% ex parte), 19% disenrolled (13% procedural), 10% pending
items.summary: CMS’s Feb. 24, 2026 All-State Medicaid and CHIP call slides report that for people due for renewal in October 2025, 71% had coverage renewed, including 49% renewed on an ex parte basis. The same CMS slides report 19% were disenrolled (13% for procedural reasons) and 10% were pending at the end of the month.

items.title: CMS enrollment snapshot: 76.8M enrolled in Medicaid/CHIP in Oct. 2025; down 260,000 from Sept. 2025
items.summary: CMS’s October 2025 Eligibility Operations and Enrollment Snapshot reports 76.8 million people enrolled in Medicaid and CHIP (69.5M Medicaid; 7.2M CHIP). CMS also reports total enrollment decreased by 260,000 (0.3%) from September 2025 to October 2025.

items.title: SSA shifts medical Continuing Disability Reviews (CDRs) to federal processing; says initial-claims backlog down from 1.26M (June 2024) to 831K (Feb. 2026)
items.summary: SSA says it is transitioning medical Continuing Disability Reviews (CDRs) from State Disability Determination Services to SSA’s federal Disability Case Review site. SSA says the DDS initial-claims backlog fell from over 1.26 million pending claims in June 2024 to 831,000 as of February 2026 (down more than 33%).

items.title: IRS: 2026 filing season opened Jan. 26; most EITC/ACTC refunds expected by March 2; filing deadline April 15
items.summary: IRS says the 2026 tax filing season opened on Jan. 26, 2026 and the deadline to file 2025 federal returns is April 15. IRS also says most refunds claiming EITC or the Additional Child Tax Credit (ACTC) are expected to be available by March 2, 2026 for direct-deposit filers with no other issues, and Where’s My Refund? should show projected deposit dates for most early EITC/ACTC filers by Feb. 21.

items.title: HUD: $10M in additional HUD-VASH administrative fee funding available; applications due April 14, 2026
items.summary: HUD’s PIH Notice 2026-01 (Revised) makes $10 million available for additional administrative fees to support the HUD-VASH program. HUD states applications must be received no later than midnight on April 14, 2026 (in the PHA’s time zone) and the funds must be used exclusively for HUD-VASH eligible administrative and related expenses.
items.source: U.S. Department of Housing and Urban Development

items.title: Federal Register: HUD interim final rule would revoke 30-day notice requirement for nonpayment evictions in public housing and PBRA; comments due April 27, 2026
items.summary: A Feb. 26, 2026 Federal Register notice says HUD issued an interim final rule revoking prior rules requiring PHAs and owners of properties with project-based rental assistance (PBRA) to provide 30-day notice before lease termination for nonpayment of rent. The notice states comments must be received by April 27, 2026.

items.title: HHS FY 2026 budget justification proposes eliminating LIHEAP: $4.025B (FY 2025) to $0 (FY 2026 request)
items.summary: HHS’s FY 2026 Administration for Children, Families, and Communities congressional justification shows LIHEAP Block Grants at $4.025 billion for FY 2024 and FY 2025, with a FY 2026 budget request of $0 (a -$4.025B change). The same document’s “Programs Proposed for Elimination” table cites concerns including limited impact on self-sufficiency and a history of fraud and abuse.
items.source: U.S. Department of Health & Human Services

items.title: Federal Register: HHS seeks comments by April 3, 2026 on extending LIHEAP Quarterly Reports information collection (OMB 0970-0589)
items.summary: A March 4, 2026 Federal Register notice (full-text XML) says HHS/ACF is requesting a 3-year extension of the LIHEAP Quarterly Reports information collection (OMB #0970-0589, expiring Oct. 31, 2025) with proposed changes intended to reduce redundancy and burden. The notice says public comments are due April 3, 2026 and directs commenters to reginfo.gov.

items.title: USDA WIC memo: FY 2026 CVV/B inflation adjustment 11.8% and April 2026 deadline to allow at least one non-fresh fruit/veg option
items.summary: USDA FNS’s WIC Policy Memorandum #2026-2 says FY 2026 cash-value voucher/benefit (CVV/B) amounts reflect an 11.8% inflation adjustment over the FY 2022 baseline, resulting in monthly amounts of $26 (children), $48 (pregnant/postpartum), and $52 (breastfeeding). The memo also says state agencies must authorize at least one additional form (frozen, canned, and/or dried) of fruits and vegetables in addition to fresh by April 2026.

items.title: Federal Student Aid: maximum Pell Grant is $7,395 for 2026–27; some students can receive up to 150% with year-round Pell
items.summary: Federal Student Aid says the maximum Federal Pell Grant award for the 2026–27 award year is $7,395. The same guidance explains that some students may be able to receive up to 150% of their yearly Pell award (often called year-round Pell) if they attend an additional term in the same award year, giving an example where a $3,000 annual award could add up to another $1,500 for summer enrollment.
